Intuitively, collapse arises when the learned representations or parameter adjustments for different tasks are fundamentally incompatible, so that merging forces destructive interference rather than synergy. In this paper, we identify and characterize the phenomenon of task-level merging collapse, where certain task combinations consistently trigger huge performance degradation across all merging methods. Through extensive experiments and statistical analysis, we demonstrate that representational incompatibility between tasks is strongly correlated with merging collapse, while parameter-space conflict metrics show minimal correlation, challenging conventional wisdom in model merging literature. We provide a theoretical explanation on this phenomenon through rate-distortion theory with a dimension-dependent bound, establishing fundamental limits on task mergeability regardless of methodology.

</details>


### [31] [Telogenesis: Goal Is All U Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09476)
*Zhuoran Deng,Yizhi Zhang,Ziyi Zhang,Wan Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores how attentional priorities can emerge internally from epistemic gaps without external rewards, validating it in different environments and demonstrating adaptive performances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether attentional priorities can emerge from the agent's internal cognitive state rather than being externally provided.

Method: A priority function is developed based on three epistemic gaps: ignorance, surprise, and staleness, validated in experiments with varied environments and using ablation to assess component necessity.

Result: Results show metric-dependent reversal between strategies, attention budget impacts detection latency differently, and learnable decay rates capture environmental volatility without supervision.

Conclusion: Epistemic gaps are sufficient to create adaptive priorities that outperform fixed strategies and recover latent environmental structure, suggesting internal mechanisms for goal-oriented behavior.

Abstract: Goal-conditioned systems assume goals are provided externally. We ask whether attentional priorities can emerge endogenously from an agent's internal cognitive state. We propose a priority function that generates observation targets from three epistemic gaps: ignorance (posterior variance), surprise (prediction error), and staleness (temporal decay of confidence in unobserved variables). We validate this in two systems: a minimal attention-allocation environment (2,000 runs) and a modular, partially observable world (500 runs). Ablation shows each component is necessary. A key finding is metric-dependent reversal: under global prediction error, coverage-based rotation wins; under change detection latency, priority-guided allocation wins, with advantage growing monotonically with dimensionality (d = -0.95 at N=48, p < 10^-6). Detection latency follows a power law in attention budget, with a steeper exponent for priority-guided allocation (0.55 vs. 0.40). When the decay rate is made learnable per variable, the system spontaneously recovers environmental volatility structure without supervision (t = 22.5, p < 10^-6). We demonstrate that epistemic gaps alone, without external reward, suffice to generate adaptive priorities that outperform fixed strategies and recover latent environmental structure.

</details>


### [32] [GenePlan: Evolving Better Generalized PDDL Plans using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09481)
*Andrew Murray,Danial Dervovic,Alberto Pozanco,Michael Cashmore*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GenePlan is a framework that uses LLM-assisted evolutionary algorithms to create generalized planners for PDDL tasks, achieving competitive performance with state-of-the-art planners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a framework that optimizes generalized planning by combining evolutionary algorithms with large language models, addressing challenges in plan generation and optimization.

Method: GenePlan integrates LLMs into evolutionary algorithms to iteratively evolve interpretable Python planners that minimize planning length across various instances of classical planning tasks.

Result: GenePlan achieves a high SAT score of 0.91, closely rivaling state-of-the-art planners and outperforming other LLM-based methods such as chain-of-thought prompting.

Conclusion: GenePlan demonstrates an effective, rapid, and cost-efficient approach to generating high-performing generalized planners, providing a valuable alternative to traditional and LLM-centric baselines.

Abstract: We present GenePlan (GENeralized Evolutionary Planner), a novel framework that leverages large language model (LLM) assisted evolutionary algorithms to generate domain-dependent generalized planners for classical planning tasks described in PDDL. By casting generalized planning as an optimization problem, GenePlan iteratively evolves interpretable Python planners that minimize plan length across diverse problem instances. In empirical evaluation across six existing benchmark domains and two new domains, GenePlan achieved an average SAT score of 0.91, closely matching the performance of the state-of-the-art planners (SAT score 0.93), and significantly outperforming other LLM-based baselines such as chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting (average SAT score 0.64). The generated planners solve new instances rapidly (average 0.49 seconds per task) and at low cost (average $1.82 per domain using GPT-4o).

</details>


### [33] [Vibe-Creation: The Epistemology of Human-AI Emergent Cognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09486)
*Ilya Levin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This study introduces the concept of the 'Third Entity,' a new cognitive formation that emerges from interactions between humans and generative AI, exploring its implications for knowledge, epistemology, and education.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to reevaluate the nature of interactions between humans and generative artificial intelligence, arguing that traditional metaphors like tool use or collaboration are insufficient to describe their distinct cognitive-epistemic formation.

Method: The study draws on multiple philosophical theories such as Peirce's semiotics, Polanyi's tacit knowledge, Simondon's individuation, Ihde's postphenomenology, and Morin's complexity theory to construct a comprehensive theoretical framework.

Result: The researchers introduce new concepts, including vibe-creation (the automation of tacit knowledge) and asymmetric emergence (novel agency balancing human intention), to explain how humans and AI interact in fundamentally new ways.

Conclusion: The paper ends by emphasizing how these theories and insights can reshape educational institutions and intellectual competence, adjusting to the transformative impact of generative AI.

Abstract: The encounter between human reasoning and generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) cannot be adequately described by inherited metaphors of tool use, augmentation, or collaborative partnership. This article argues that such interactions produce a qualitatively distinct cognitive-epistemic formation, designated here as the Third Entity: an emergent, transient structure that arises from the transductive coupling of two ontologically incommensurable modes of cognition. Drawing on Peirce semiotics, Polanyi theory of tacit knowledge, Simondon philosophy of individuation, Ihde postphenomenology, and Morin complexity theory, we develop a multi-layered theoretical account of this formation. We introduce the concept of vibe-creation to designate the pre-reflective cognitive mode through which the Third Entity navigates high-dimensional semantic space and argue that this mode constitutes the automation of tacit knowledge - a development with far-reaching consequences for epistemology, the philosophy of mind, and educational theory. We further propose the notion of asymmetric emergence to characterize the agency of the Third Entity: genuinely novel and irreducible, yet anchored in human intentional responsibility. The article concludes by examining the implications of this theoretical framework for the transformation of educational institutions and the redefinition of intellectual competence in the age of GenAI.

</details>


### [34] [Enhancing Debunking Effectiveness through LLM-based Personality Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09533)
*Pietro Dell'Oglio,Alessandro Bondielli,Francesco Marcelloni,Lucia C. Passaro*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The study explores how Large Language Models (LLMs) can create personalized fake news debunking messages tailored to Big Five personality traits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the effectiveness of fake news debunking by tailoring messages to individuals' personality traits, thus making them more persuasive.

Method: The authors prompt LLMs with persona-based inputs representing Big Five traits to create personalized debunking messages. They also use LLM evaluators to simulate human panel evaluation for assessing persuasiveness.

Result: Personalized messages are generally perceived as more persuasive than generic ones. Traits like Openness increase persuadability, while Neuroticism tends to lower it. Variation in LLM evaluators highlights the need for using multiple models.

Conclusion: The study offers a practical methodology for personalized fake news debunking using LLMs but also raises ethical concerns regarding its use and implications.

Abstract: This study proposes a novel methodology for generating personalized fake news debunking messages by prompting Large Language Models (LLMs) with persona-based inputs aligned to the Big Five personality traits: Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Neuroticism, and Openness. Our approach guides LLMs to transform generic debunking content into personalized versions tailored to specific personality profiles. To assess the effectiveness of these transformations, we employ a separate LLM as an automated evaluator simulating corresponding personality traits, thereby eliminating the need for costly human evaluation panels. Our results show that personalized messages are generally seen as more persuasive than generic ones. We also find that traits like Openness tend to increase persuadability, while Neuroticism can lower it. Differences between LLM evaluators suggest that using multiple models provides a clearer picture. Overall, this work demonstrates a practical way to create more targeted debunking messages exploiting LLMs, while also raising important ethical questions about how such technology might be used.

</details>


### [35] [Context Engineering: From Prompts to Corporate Multi-Agent Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09619)
*Vera V. Vishnyakova*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces context engineering (CE) and three related disciplines (PE, IE, SE) as a framework for building and managing multi-agent AI systems at scale, addressing challenges in deploying agentic AI in enterprises.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI systems evolve, the current reliance on prompt engineering (PE) is insufficient for the complexity of autonomous multi-agent systems. Enterprises need frameworks for crafting decision-making environments as they face deployment challenges in scaling these systems.

Method: Proposes CE as a new discipline of designing the informational environment for AI agents, along with IE and SE for encoding organizational goals and standards. Introduces quality criteria (relevance, sufficiency, isolation, economy, provenance) and a pyramid maturity model. Draws on industry cases, frameworks, and vendor architectures.

Result: Identifies a gap in enterprise maturity for agentic AI deployment. Points to the Klarna case as evidence of contextual and intentional deficits hindering scalable AI systems.

Conclusion: CE, alongside IE and SE, forms an essential part of a maturity model for agent engineering. Mastery over these disciplines is vital for scaling agentic AI systems effectively and aligning them with organizational goals.

Abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI) systems evolve from stateless chatbots to autonomous multi-step agents, prompt engineering (PE), the discipline of crafting individual queries, proves necessary but insufficient. This paper introduces context engineering (CE) as a standalone discipline concerned with designing, structuring, and managing the entire informational environment in which an AI agent makes decisions. Drawing on vendor architectures (Google ADK, Anthropic, LangChain), current academic work (ACE framework, Google DeepMind's intelligent delegation), enterprise research (Deloitte, 2026; KPMG, 2026), and the author's experience building a multi-agent system, the paper proposes five context quality criteria: relevance, sufficiency, isolation, economy, and provenance, and frames context as the agent's operating system. Two higher-order disciplines follow. Intent engineering (IE) encodes organizational goals, values, and trade-off hierarchies into agent infrastructure. Specification engineering (SE) creates a machine-readable corpus of corporate policies and standards enabling autonomous operation of multi-agent systems at scale. Together these four disciplines form a cumulative pyramid maturity model of agent engineering, in which each level subsumes the previous one as a necessary foundation. Enterprise data reveals a gap: while 75% of enterprises plan agentic AI deployment within two years (Deloitte, 2026), deployment has surged and retreated as organizations confront scaling complexity (KPMG, 2026). The Klarna case illustrates a dual deficit, contextual and intentional. Whoever controls the agent's context controls its behavior; whoever controls its intent controls its strategy; whoever controls its specifications controls its scale.

</details>


### [36] [PRECEPT: Planning Resilience via Experience, Context Engineering & Probing Trajectories A Unified Framework for Test-Time Adaptation with Compositional Rule Learning and Pareto-Guided Prompt Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09641)
*Arash Shahmansoori*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents PRECEPT, a framework aimed at addressing limitations in LLM agents related to retrieval degradation, compositional reliability, and stale/adversarial knowledge handling. It shows notable improvements in retrieval accuracy, compositional reasoning, continuous learning, and robustness under adversarial conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents often suffer from retrieval inaccuracies as condition count increases, struggle to compose learned rules, and lack mechanisms to detect and handle stale or adversarial knowledge.

Method: The paper introduces PRECEPT, which includes three components: exact-match rule retrieval to eliminate interpretation errors, conflict-aware memory for drift adaptation and conflict resolution, and COMPASS for optimizing prompts through a Pareto-guided prompt-evolution loop.

Result: PRECEPT achieves significant gains, including a +41.1pp first-try advantage, +33.3pp compositional generalization, and better robustness under adversarial conditions, all with 61% fewer steps. Results are statistically significant.

Conclusion: The PRECEPT framework effectively addresses knowledge retrieval, compositional reasoning, and adaptation challenges in LLM agents, demonstrating large improvements in accuracy, learning efficiency, and handling of adversarial or stale knowledge.

Abstract: LLM agents that store knowledge as natural language suffer steep retrieval degradation as condition count grows, often struggle to compose learned rules reliably, and typically lack explicit mechanisms to detect stale or adversarial knowledge. We introduce PRECEPT, a unified framework for test-time adaptation with three tightly coupled components: (1) deterministic exact-match rule retrieval over structured condition keys, (2) conflict-aware memory with Bayesian source reliability and threshold-based rule invalidation, and (3) COMPASS, a Pareto-guided prompt-evolution outer loop. Exact retrieval eliminates partial-match interpretation errors on the deterministic path (0% by construction, vs 94.4% under Theorem~B.6's independence model at N=10) and supports compositional stacking through a semantic tier hierarchy; conflict-aware memory resolves static--dynamic disagreements and supports drift adaptation; COMPASS evaluates prompts through the same end-to-end execution pipeline.
  Results (9--10 seeds): PRECEPT achieves a +41.1pp first-try advantage over Full Reflexion (d>1.9), +33.3pp compositional generalization (d=1.55), 100% $P_1$ on 2-way logistics compositions (d=2.64), +40--55pp continuous learning gains, strong eventual robustness under adversarial static knowledge (100% logistics with adversarial SK active; partial recovery on integration), +55.0pp drift recovery (d=0.95, p=0.031), and 61% fewer steps. Core comparisons are statistically significant, often at p<0.001.

</details>


### [37] [MiniAppBench: Evaluating the Shift from Text to Interactive HTML Responses in LLM-Powered Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09652)
*Zuhao Zhang,Chengyue Yu,Yuante Li,Chenyi Zhuang,Linjian Mo,Shuai Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces MiniAppBench, a benchmark for evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating interactive, principle-driven MiniApps, and presents MiniAppEval, an agentic evaluation framework for assessing these MiniApps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs excel in static text and code generation, there is a lack of benchmarks for generating dynamic, interactive applications that align with real-world principles, creating a gap in human-AI interaction evaluation.

Method: The study develops MiniAppBench, a benchmark distilling 500 real-world-based tasks in six domains, and MiniAppEval, a browser automation framework that evaluates using human-like exploratory testing across Intention, Static, and Dynamic dimensions.

Result: Experiments show that LLMs face significant challenges in producing high-quality MiniApps, and that MiniAppEval aligns well with human judgment, offering a reliable evaluation standard.

Conclusion: This work identifies limitations of LLMs in interactive MiniApp generation and establishes MiniAppBench and MiniAppEval as crucial tools for advancing research in this area.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) in code generation, human-AI interaction is evolving from static text responses to dynamic, interactive HTML-based applications, which we term MiniApps. These applications require models to not only render visual interfaces but also construct customized interaction logic that adheres to real-world principles. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on algorithmic correctness or static layout reconstruction, failing to capture the capabilities required for this new paradigm. To address this gap, we introduce MiniAppBench, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate principle-driven, interactive application generation. Sourced from a real-world application with 10M+ generations, MiniAppBench distills 500 tasks across six domains (e.g., Games, Science, and Tools). Furthermore, to tackle the challenge of evaluating open-ended interactions where no single ground truth exists, we propose MiniAppEval, an agentic evaluation framework. Leveraging browser automation, it performs human-like exploratory testing to systematically assess applications across three dimensions: Intention, Static, and Dynamic. Our experiments reveal that current LLMs still face significant challenges in generating high-quality MiniApps, while MiniAppEval demonstrates high alignment with human judgment, establishing a reliable standard for future research. Our code is available in github.com/MiniAppBench.

</details>


### [38] [Logics-Parsing-Omni Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09677)
*Xin An,Jingyi Cai,Xiangyang Chen,Huayao Liu,Peiting Liu,Peng Wang,Bei Yang,Xiuwen Zhu,Yongfan Chen,Baoyu Hou,Shuzhao Li,Weidong Ren,Fan Yang,Jiangtao Zhang,Xiaoxiao Xu,Lin Qu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Omni Parsing framework to unify and streamline multimodal data parsing, using progressive parsing and an evidence-based approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in fragmented task definitions and heterogeneity in unstructured multimodal data (e.g., documents, images, and audio-visual inputs).

Method: The authors propose a hierarchical framework integrating: 1) Holistic Detection for spatial-temporal grounding; 2) Fine-grained Recognition for symbolization and attribute extraction; 3) Multi-level Interpreting for reasoning chain construction. The evidence anchoring mechanism ensures alignment between high-level and low-level data.

Result: The paper presents the Logics-Parsing-Omni model, capable of converting audio-visual signals into standardized knowledge. Experimental results show improved model reliability due to the synergy of fine-grained perception and high-level cognition.

Conclusion: The proposed Omni Parsing framework effectively enhances multimodal parsing and cognition capabilities, and releases related datasets, models, and a benchmark for assessing these tasks.

Abstract: Addressing the challenges of fragmented task definitions and the heterogeneity of unstructured data in multimodal parsing, this paper proposes the Omni Parsing framework. This framework establishes a Unified Taxonomy covering documents, images, and audio-visual streams, introducing a progressive parsing paradigm that bridges perception and cognition. Specifically, the framework integrates three hierarchical levels: 1) Holistic Detection, which achieves precise spatial-temporal grounding of objects or events to establish a geometric baseline for perception; 2) Fine-grained Recognition, which performs symbolization (e.g., OCR/ASR) and attribute extraction on localized objects to complete structured entity parsing; and 3) Multi-level Interpreting, which constructs a reasoning chain from local semantics to global logic. A pivotal advantage of this framework is its evidence anchoring mechanism, which enforces a strict alignment between high-level semantic descriptions and low-level facts. This enables ``evidence-based'' logical induction, transforming unstructured signals into standardized knowledge that is locatable, enumerable, and traceable. Building on this foundation, we constructed a standardized dataset and released the Logics-Parsing-Omni model, which successfully converts complex audio-visual signals into machine-readable structured knowledge. Experiments demonstrate that fine-grained perception and high-level cognition are synergistic, effectively enhancing model reliability. Furthermore, to quantitatively evaluate these capabilities, we introduce OmniParsingBench. Code, models and the benchmark are released at https://github.com/alibaba/Logics-Parsing/tree/master/Logics-Parsing-Omni.

</details>


### [39] [OOD-MMSafe: Advancing MLLM Safety from Harmful Intent to Hidden Consequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09706)
*Ming Wen,Kun Yang,Jingyu Zhang,Yuxuan Liu,shiwen cui,Shouling Ji,Xingjun Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper discusses shifting safety in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) from malicious intent alignment to consequence-driven safety and introduces a benchmark to evaluate latent hazard identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address shortcomings in existing safety paradigms for MLLMs by focusing on latent hazards and moving towards robust, consequence-aware deployment.

Method: Developed OOD-MMSafe benchmark to measure contextual hazard detection and proposed the CASPO framework for safety policy optimization through dynamic reward mechanisms.

Result: CASPO framework significantly reduced risk identification failure rates to 7.3% and 5.7% for different models, while maintaining effectiveness.

Conclusion: Consequence-driven safety enhances the safety alignment for MLLMs by addressing latent hazards and overcoming failures associated with static alignment approaches.

Abstract: While safety alignment for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has gained significant attention, current paradigms primarily target malicious intent or situational violations. We propose shifting the safety frontier toward consequence-driven safety, a paradigm essential for the robust deployment of autonomous and embodied agents. To formalize this shift, we introduce OOD-MMSafe, a benchmark comprising 455 curated query-image pairs designed to evaluate a model's ability to identify latent hazards within context-dependent causal chains. Our analysis reveals a pervasive causal blindness among frontier models, with the highest 67.5% failure rate in high-capacity closed-source models, and identifies a preference ceiling where static alignment yields format-centric failures rather than improved safety reasoning as model capacity grows. To address these bottlenecks, we develop the Consequence-Aware Safety Policy Optimization (CASPO) framework, which integrates the model's intrinsic reasoning as a dynamic reference for token-level self-distillation rewards. Experimental results demonstrate that CASPO significantly enhances consequence projection, reducing the failure ratio of risk identification to 7.3% for Qwen2.5-VL-7B and 5.7% for Qwen3-VL-4B while maintaining overall effectiveness.

</details>


### [40] [Does the Question Really Matter? Training-Free Data Selection for Vision-Language SFT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09715)
*Peng Sun,Huawen Shen,Yi Ban,Tianfan Fu,Yanbo Wang,Yuqiang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces CVS, a training-free method for selecting high-quality samples for visual instruction tuning in vision-language models, enhancing joint reasoning and computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current data selection methods for visual instruction tuning fail to ensure cross-modal reasoning and rely heavily on training-intensive proxies, limiting effective multimodal learning.

Method: CVS uses a frozen vision-language large model to evaluate the discrepancy in answer validity when conditioned on a question, identifying samples requiring joint reasoning while filtering noise.

Result: CVS achieves higher performance with significantly less data, outperforming full-data training by up to 4.8% on specific datasets and reducing computational cost by up to 44.4% compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: CVS effectively identifies high-quality multimodal data samples, improving performance and reducing data and computational requirements for vision-language instruction tuning.

Abstract: Visual instruction tuning is crucial for improving vision-language large models (VLLMs). However, many samples can be solved via linguistic patterns or common-sense shortcuts, without genuine cross-modal reasoning, limiting the effectiveness of multimodal learning. Prior data selection methods often rely on costly proxy model training and focus on difficulty or diversity, failing to capture a sample's true contribution to vision-language joint reasoning. In this paper, we propose CVS, a training-free data selection method based on the insight that, for high-quality multimodal samples, introducing the question should substantially alter the model's assessment of answer validity given an image. CVS leverages a frozen VLLM as an evaluator and measures the discrepancy in answer validity with and without conditioning on the question, enabling the identification of samples that require vision-language joint reasoning while filtering semantic-conflict noise. Experiments on Vision-Flan and The Cauldron show that CVS achieves solid performance across datasets. On Vision-Flan, CVS outperforms full-data training by 3.5% and 4.8% using only 10% and 15% of the data, respectively, and remains robust on the highly heterogeneous Cauldron dataset. Moreover, CVS reduces computational cost by 17.3% and 44.4% compared to COINCIDE and XMAS.

</details>


### [41] [AutoAgent: Evolving Cognition and Elastic Memory Orchestration for Adaptive Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09716)
*Xiaoxing Wang,Ning Liao,Shikun Wei,Chen Tang,Feiyu Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoAgent is a self-evolving multi-agent framework designed to enhance adaptability in dynamic environments through evolving cognition, real-time decision-making, and elastic memory orchestration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of autonomous agents struggling with long-term experiential learning, static cognition, and inefficient context usage in non-stationary environments.

Method: AutoAgent integrates three key components: evolving cognition, real-time contextual decision-making, and an Elastic Memory Orchestrator to dynamically manage interaction history. It aligns actions with outcomes in a closed-loop manner without retraining.

Result: AutoAgent demonstrated improvements in task success, tool-use efficiency, and collaborative robustness in benchmarks across reasoning, tool use, and embodied tasks.

Conclusion: This framework offers a practical path for creating adaptable, autonomous agents capable of both experiential learning and reliable real-time decision-making in dynamic settings.

Abstract: Autonomous agent frameworks still struggle to reconcile long-term experiential learning with real-time, context-sensitive decision-making. In practice, this gap appears as static cognition, rigid workflow dependence, and inefficient context usage, which jointly limit adaptability in open-ended and non-stationary environments. To address these limitations, we present AutoAgent, a self-evolving multi-agent framework built on three tightly coupled components: evolving cognition, on-the-fly contextual decision-making, and elastic memory orchestration. At the core of AutoAgent, each agent maintains structured prompt-level cognition over tools, self-capabilities, peer expertise, and task knowledge. During execution, this cognition is combined with live task context to select actions from a unified space that includes tool calls, LLM-based generation, and inter-agent requests. To support efficient long-horizon reasoning, an Elastic Memory Orchestrator dynamically organizes interaction history by preserving raw records, compressing redundant trajectories, and constructing reusable episodic abstractions, thereby reducing token overhead while retaining decision-critical evidence. These components are integrated through a closed-loop cognitive evolution process that aligns intended actions with observed outcomes to continuously update cognition and expand reusable skills, without external retraining. Empirical results across retrieval-augmented reasoning, tool-augmented agent benchmarks, and embodied task environments show that AutoAgent consistently improves task success, tool-use efficiency, and collaborative robustness over static and memory-augmented baselines. Overall, AutoAgent provides a unified and practical foundation for adaptive autonomous agents that must learn from experience while making reliable context-aware decisions in dynamic environments.

</details>


### [42] [World2Mind: Cognition Toolkit for Allocentric Spatial Reasoning in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09774)
*Shouwei Ruan,Bin Wang,Zhenyu Wu,Qihui Zhu,Yuxiang Zhang,Hang Su,Yubin Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: World2Mind introduces a training-free spatial intelligence toolkit that enhances the spatial reasoning of existing Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs) by leveraging 3D reconstruction, instance segmentation, and spatial cognitive mappings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MFMs struggle with robust spatial reasoning due to overfitting to 3D data or a focus on 2D perception, limiting their accuracy and generalization.

Method: World2Mind employs tools like 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation to build structured spatial cognitive maps and uses an Allocentric-Spatial Tree (AST) for accurate top-down modeling of spatial layouts. It includes a three-stage reasoning chain to address 3D reconstruction inaccuracies.

Result: World2Mind improves the performance of advanced models like GPT-5.2 by 5%-18% and enables text-only models to approximate the 3D reasoning capabilities of multimodal models with significant accuracy.

Conclusion: World2Mind significantly enhances the spatial reasoning capacities of MFMs, offering a robust toolkit that bridges the gap between text-only and multimodal model performance in spatial tasks.

Abstract: Achieving robust spatial reasoning remains a fundamental challenge for current Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs). Existing methods either overfit statistical shortcuts via 3D grounding data or remain confined to 2D visual perception, limiting both spatial reasoning accuracy and generalization in unseen scenarios. Inspired by the spatial cognitive mapping mechanisms of biological intelligence, we propose World2Mind, a training-free spatial intelligence toolkit. At its core, World2Mind leverages 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation models to construct structured spatial cognitive maps, empowering MFMs to proactively acquire targeted spatial knowledge regarding interested landmarks and routes of interest. To provide robust geometric-topological priors, World2Mind synthesizes an Allocentric-Spatial Tree (AST) that uses elliptical parameters to model the top-down layout of landmarks accurately. To mitigate the inherent inaccuracies of 3D reconstruction, we introduce a three-stage reasoning chain comprising tool invocation assessment, modality-decoupled cue collection, and geometry-semantics interwoven reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that World2Mind boosts the performance of frontier models, such as GPT-5.2, by 5%~18%. Astonishingly, relying solely on the AST-structured text, purely text-only foundation models can perform complex 3D spatial reasoning, achieving performance approaching that of advanced multimodal models.

</details>


### [43] [Quantifying the Necessity of Chain of Thought through Opaque Serial Depth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09786)
*Jonah Brown-Cohen,David Lindner,Rohin Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates the tendency of large language models (LLMs) to externalize reasoning via the chain of thought, introducing the concept of opaque serial depth to measure non-externalized reasoning ability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to formalize why and how LLMs externalize thought processes in reasoning, and quantify the capability of models to perform non-externalized reasoning.

Method: They introduced the concept of opaque serial depth, computed numeric upper bounds for Gemma 3 models, derived asymptotic results, and developed an automated tool for measuring opaque serial depth in various architectures.

Result: They found that Mixture-of-Experts models likely have lower opaque serial depth compared to dense models and demonstrated the application of opaque serial depth as a metric.

Conclusion: Opaque serial depth provides a framework for understanding models' potential for non-externalized reasoning and advances understanding of reasoning within neural network architectures.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) tend to externalize their reasoning in their chain of thought, making the chain of thought a good target for monitoring. This is partially an inherent feature of the Transformer architecture: sufficiently long serial cognition must pass through the chain of thought (Korbak et al., 2025). We formalize this argument through the notion of opaque serial depth, given by the length of the longest computation that can be done without the use of interpretable intermediate steps like chain of thought. Given this formalization, we compute numeric upper bounds on the opaque serial depth of Gemma 3 models, as well as asymptotic results for additional architectures beyond standard LLMs. We also open-source an automated method that can calculate upper bounds on the opaque serial depth of arbitrary neural networks, and use it to demonstrate that Mixture-of-Experts models likely have lower depth than dense models. Overall, our results suggest that opaque serial depth is a useful tool for understanding the potential for models to do significant reasoning that is not externalized.

</details>


### [44] [LCA: Local Classifier Alignment for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09888)
*Tung Tran,Danilo Vasconcellos Vargas,Khoat Than*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel approach, Local Classifier Alignment (LCA), to address catastrophic forgetting in continuous learning by aligning the classifier with the backbone network, achieving improved generalization and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this work is to enable intelligent systems to learn continuously under changing environments without succumbing to catastrophic forgetting, a common issue in continuous learning.

Method: The paper proposes a Local Classifier Alignment (LCA) loss function for better alignment between the classifier and backbone, paired with a model merging approach to enhance continual learning.

Result: The proposed method achieves leading performance on standard benchmarks, often outperforming state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin.

Conclusion: Combining LCA with a model merging approach addresses the mismatch between classifiers and backbones in continual learning, providing improved performance and robustness.

Abstract: A fundamental requirement for intelligent systems is the ability to learn continuously under changing environments. However, models trained in this regime often suffer from catastrophic forgetting. Leveraging pre-trained models has recently emerged as a promising solution, since their generalized feature extractors enable faster and more robust adaptation. While some earlier works mitigate forgetting by fine-tuning only on the first task, this approach quickly deteriorates as the number of tasks grows and the data distributions diverge. More recent research instead seeks to consolidate task knowledge into a unified backbone, or adapting the backbone as new tasks arrive. However, such approaches may create a (potential) \textit{mismatch} between task-specific classifiers and the adapted backbone. To address this issue, we propose a novel \textit{Local Classifier Alignment} (LCA) loss to better align the classifier with backbone. Theoretically, we show that this LCA loss can enable the classifier to not only generalize well for all observed tasks, but also improve robustness. Furthermore, we develop a complete solution for continual learning, following the model merging approach and using LCA. Extensive experiments on several standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method often achieves leading performance, sometimes surpasses the state-of-the-art methods with a large margin.

</details>


### [45] [Influencing LLM Multi-Agent Dialogue via Policy-Parameterized Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09890)
*Hongbo Bo,Jingyu Hu,Weiru Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores parameterized prompts as lightweight policies for controlling LLM-based multi-agent dialogue behaviours, demonstrating their effectiveness in influencing discussions without additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by the lack of a principled policy approach in current LLM multi-agent behavioural studies, the authors aim to investigate an efficient method for influencing dialogue processes without relying on computationally heavy methodologies like reinforcement learning.

Method: The authors develop a framework where prompts are treated as actions that are dynamically constructed based on the agent's state through five components, creating a parameterized policy. They evaluate this approach across five dialogue indicators.

Result: The approach demonstrates that parameterized prompts effectively influence dialogue dynamics by assessing their impact on responsiveness, rebuttal, evidence usage, non-repetition, and stance shifts across different scenarios.

Conclusion: The study concludes that policy-parameterized prompts are a simple yet effective mechanism for controlling LLM-based multi-agent systems and can advance research in social simulation by offering a new policy framework.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as a new paradigm for multi-agent systems. However, existing research on the behaviour of LLM-based multi-agents relies on ad hoc prompts and lacks a principled policy perspective. Different from reinforcement learning, we investigate whether prompt-as-action can be parameterized so as to construct a lightweight policy which consists of a sequence of state-action pairs to influence conversational behaviours without training. Our framework regards prompts as actions executed by LLMs, and dynamically constructs prompts through five components based on the current state of the agent. To test the effectiveness of parameterized control, we evaluated the dialogue flow based on five indicators: responsiveness, rebuttal, evidence usage, non-repetition, and stance shift. We conduct experiments using different LLM-driven agents in two discussion scenarios related to the general public and show that prompt parameterization can influence the dialogue dynamics. This result shows that policy-parameterised prompts offer a simple and effective mechanism to influence the dialogue process, which will help the research of multi-agent systems in the direction of social simulation.

</details>


### [46] [MedMASLab: A Unified Orchestration Framework for Benchmarking Multimodal Medical Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09909)
*Yunhang Qian,Xiaobin Hu,Jiaquan Yu,Siyang Xin,Xiaokun Chen,Jiangning Zhang,Peng-Tao Jiang,Jiawei Liu,Hongwei Bran Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces MedMASLab, a unified framework for multimodal medical multi-agent systems (MAS), addressing standardization, evaluation, and benchmarking issues in the field.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome architectural fragmentation, inconsistent evaluation, and lack of benchmarks in medical multi-agent systems, enabling more unified and efficient clinical decision support.

Method: Proposes MedMASLab, including a standardized agent communication protocol, an automated clinical reasoning evaluator using vision-language models, and a large benchmark covering multiple organ systems and diseases.

Result: Reveals MAS capabilities in improving reasoning while highlighting their fragility across sub-domains, and provides rigorous analyses for future improvements in autonomous clinical systems.

Conclusion: MedMASLab sets a new technical baseline for multimodal medical MAS with standardized evaluation and benchmarking, driving forward autonomous clinical decision-making research.

Abstract: While Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) show potential for complex clinical decision support, the field remains hindered by architectural fragmentation and the lack of standardized multimodal integration. Current medical MAS research suffers from non-uniform data ingestion pipelines, inconsistent visual-reasoning evaluation, and a lack of cross-specialty benchmarking. To address these challenges, we present MedMASLab, a unified framework and benchmarking platform for multimodal medical multi-agent systems. MedMASLab introduces: (1) A standardized multimodal agent communication protocol that enables seamless integration of 11 heterogeneous MAS architectures across 24 medical modalities. (2) An automated clinical reasoning evaluator, a zero-shot semantic evaluation paradigm that overcomes the limitations of lexical string-matching by leveraging large vision-language models to verify diagnostic logic and visual grounding. (3) The most extensive benchmark to date, spanning 11 organ systems and 473 diseases, standardizing data from 11 clinical benchmarks. Our systematic evaluation reveals a critical domain-specific performance gap: while MAS improves reasoning depth, current architectures exhibit significant fragility when transitioning between specialized medical sub-domains. We provide a rigorous ablation of interaction mechanisms and cost-performance trade-offs, establishing a new technical baseline for future autonomous clinical systems. The source code and data is publicly available at: https://github.com/NUS-Project/MedMASLab/

</details>


### [47] [PathMem: Toward Cognition-Aligned Memory Transformation for Pathology MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09943)
*Jinyue Li,Yuci Liang,Qiankun Li,Xinheng Lyu,Jiayu Qian,Huabao Chen,Kun Wang,Zhigang Zeng,Anil Anthony Bharath,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The research presents PathMem, a memory-based multimodal model to enhance pathology diagnostics by integrating structured knowledge and improving reasoning capabilities, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in computational pathology diagnostics where current models fail to consistently integrate structured domain knowledge while maintaining interpretability during reasoning.

Method: Developed PathMem, a multimodal framework that utilizes long-term memory (LTM) for structured knowledge and a Memory Transformer for dynamic memory activation and knowledge grounding in reasoning tasks.

Result: PathMem surpasses baseline models, boasting improvements such as 12.8% higher WSI-Precision, a 10.1% rise in WSI-Relevance, and up to 9.7% better open-ended diagnosis performance.

Conclusion: PathMem provides a robust, interpretable, and efficient solution for diagnostic reasoning in computational pathology by using memory-centric models informed by hierarchical human cognitive processes.

Abstract: Computational pathology demands both visual pattern recognition and dynamic integration of structured domain knowledge, including taxonomy, grading criteria, and clinical evidence. In practice, diagnostic reasoning requires linking morphological evidence with formal diagnostic and grading criteria. Although multimodal large language models (MLLMs) demonstrate strong vision language reasoning capabilities, they lack explicit mechanisms for structured knowledge integration and interpretable memory control. As a result, existing models struggle to consistently incorporate pathology-specific diagnostic standards during reasoning. Inspired by the hierarchical memory process of human pathologists, we propose PathMem, a memory-centric multimodal framework for pathology MLLMs. PathMem organizes structured pathology knowledge as a long-term memory (LTM) and introduces a Memory Transformer that models the dynamic transition from LTM to working memory (WM) through multimodal memory activation and context-aware knowledge grounding, enabling context-aware memory refinement for downstream reasoning. PathMem achieves SOTA performance across benchmarks, improving WSI-Bench report generation (12.8% WSI-Precision, 10.1% WSI-Relevance) and open-ended diagnosis by 9.7% and 8.9% over prior WSI-based models.

</details>


### [48] [The Confidence Gate Theorem: When Should Ranked Decision Systems Abstain?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09947)
*Ronald Doku*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies confidence-based abstention in ranked decision systems, identifying conditions for its effectiveness and exploring the challenges caused by structural and contextual uncertainties. It validates findings across three domains and suggests diagnostics for practical deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from addressing when and how confidence-based abstention can improve ranked decision systems' outputs, such as recommendations or clinical triaging, and understanding the factors causing its failure.

Method: The paper uses theoretical conditions like rank-alignment and no-inversion zones, separating structural and contextual uncertainties. Empirical validation is conducted on datasets from collaborative filtering (MovieLens), e-commerce, and clinical triage (MIMIC-IV).

Result: Empirical results show structural uncertainty aligns well with monotonic abstention gains, while contextual uncertainty poses challenges that existing context-aware methods only partially address. Exception labels are also shown to degrade under distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Practitioners should analyze uncertainty types (structural vs. contextual) and validate theoretical conditions on data before deploying confidence-based abstention systems. Matching confidence signals to the primary uncertainty type improves reliability but contextual issues remain challenging.

Abstract: Ranked decision systems -- recommenders, ad auctions, clinical triage queues -- must decide when to intervene in ranked outputs and when to abstain. We study when confidence-based abstention monotonically improves decision quality, and when it fails. The formal conditions are simple: rank-alignment and no inversion zones. The substantive contribution is identifying why these conditions hold or fail: the distinction between structural uncertainty (missing data, e.g., cold-start) and contextual uncertainty (missing context, e.g., temporal drift). Empirically, we validate this distinction across three domains: collaborative filtering (MovieLens, 3 distribution shifts), e-commerce intent detection (RetailRocket, Criteo, Yoochoose), and clinical pathway triage (MIMIC-IV). Structural uncertainty produces near-monotonic abstention gains in all domains; structurally grounded confidence signals (observation counts) fail under contextual drift, producing as many monotonicity violations as random abstention on our MovieLens temporal split. Context-aware alternatives -- ensemble disagreement and recency features -- substantially narrow the gap (reducing violations from 3 to 1--2) but do not fully restore monotonicity, suggesting that contextual uncertainty poses qualitatively different challenges. Exception labels defined from residuals degrade substantially under distribution shift (AUC drops from 0.71 to 0.61--0.62 across three splits), providing a clean negative result against the common practice of exception-based intervention. The results provide a practical deployment diagnostic: check C1 and C2 on held-out data before deploying a confidence gate, and match the confidence signal to the dominant uncertainty type.

</details>


### [49] [Think Before You Lie: How Reasoning Improves Honesty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09957)
*Ann Yuan,Asma Ghandeharioun,Carter Blum,Alicia Machado,Jessica Hoffmann,Daphne Ippolito,Martin Wattenberg,Lucas Dixon,Katja Filippova*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The study explores the conditions influencing deceptive behavior in large language models, finding that reasoning boosts honesty and deceptive regions in representational space are less stable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the mechanisms behind deceptive behavior in large language models.

Method: The researchers used a novel dataset of moral trade-offs to analyze reasoning effects and examined the stability of deceptive vs. honest regions within representational spaces.

Result: Reasoning increases honesty in LLMs unlike in humans, and this behavior is linked to the stability of honest versus deceptive regions in the representational space.

Conclusion: Reasoning enhances honesty in LLMs by navigating a biased representational space favoring stable, honest defaults.

Abstract: While existing evaluations of large language models (LLMs) measure deception rates, the underlying conditions that give rise to deceptive behavior are poorly understood. We investigate this question using a novel dataset of realistic moral trade-offs where honesty incurs variable costs. Contrary to humans, who tend to become less honest given time to deliberate (Capraro, 2017; Capraro et al., 2019), we find that reasoning consistently increases honesty across scales and for several LLM families. This effect is not only a function of the reasoning content, as reasoning traces are often poor predictors of final behaviors. Rather, we show that the underlying geometry of the representational space itself contributes to the effect. Namely, we observe that deceptive regions within this space are metastable: deceptive answers are more easily destabilized by input paraphrasing, output resampling, and activation noise than honest ones. We interpret the effect of reasoning in this vein: generating deliberative tokens as part of moral reasoning entails the traversal of a biased representational space, ultimately nudging the model toward its more stable, honest defaults.

</details>


<div id='cs.AR'></div>

# cs.AR [[Back]](#toc)

### [50] [A Hybrid Residue Floating Numerical Architecture with Formal Error Bounds for High Throughput FPGA Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08712)
*Mostafa Darvishi*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Hybrid Residue Floating Numerical Architecture (HRFNA), an efficient numerical system for FPGAs combining residue arithmetic with scaling, achieving higher throughput and efficiency compared to traditional floating-point methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiency of traditional floating-point arithmetic on FPGAs by developing a more efficient numerical system with wide dynamic range and predictable error behavior.

Method: Developed HRFNA using residue arithmetic and exponent-based scaling, implemented a full FPGA microarchitecture with pipelined modular arithmetic, exponent management, and normalization engines.

Result: HRFNA showed up to 2.4x higher throughput, 38-55% LUT reduction, and up to 1.9x greater energy efficiency compared to IEEE 754 FP32 baselines, with bounded numerical error.

Conclusion: HRFNA strikes a balance between numerical stability, dynamic range, and hardware efficiency, making it ideal for FPGA-based applications in scientific and CAD computations.

Abstract: Floating point arithmetic is costly on FPGA platforms due to wide datapaths, normalization, and carry propagation, motivating alternative numerical representations that improve throughput and efficiency. This paper presents the Hybrid Residue Floating Numerical Architecture (HRFNA), a fully specified numerical system that combines carry-free residue arithmetic with lightweight exponent-based scaling to achieve wide dynamic range, predictable error behavior, and efficient FPGA implementation. HRFNA is developed with a rigorous mathematical foundation: the hybrid number space is formally defined, correctness of arithmetic and normalization is proven, and explicit absolute and relative error bounds are derived, confining rounding to infrequent normalization events. A complete FPGA microarchitecture is presented, featuring deeply pipelined modular arithmetic, exponent management, and a CRT-based normalization engine that sustains an initiation interval of one cycle under steady-state operation. Application-level evaluation on dot products, dense matrix multiplication, and iterative Runge-Kutta ODE solvers demonstrates stable numerical behavior over long computation sequences. Implemented on a Xilinx Zynq UltraScale Plus ZCU104, HRFNA achieves up to 2.4 times higher throughput, 38 to 55 percent LUT reduction, and up to 1.9 times energy efficiency improvement compared to IEEE 754 FP32 baselines, while maintaining bounded numerical error. Comparative analysis shows that HRFNA occupies a previously unexplored design point between numerical stability, dynamic range, and hardware efficiency, making it well suited for FPGA-centric scientific and CAD-relevant computation.

</details>


### [51] [Unveiling the Potential of Quantization with MXFP4: Strategies for Quantization Error Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08713)
*Jatin Chhugani,Geonhwa Jeong,Bor-Yiing Su,Yunjie Pan,Hanmei Yang,Aayush Ankit,Jiecao Yu,Summer Deng,Yunqing Chen,Nadathur Satish,Changkyu Kim*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper proposes two software techniques, Overflow-Aware Scaling (OAS) and Macro Block Scaling (MBS), to enhance accuracy of MXFP4 quantization and reduce its accuracy gap with NVFP4 significantly, enabling efficient large-scale inference while retaining hardware efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the accuracy limitations of MXFP4, which restricts its adoption despite its hardware efficiency compared to NVIDIA's NVFP4 in low-precision computation.

Method: Two techniques are introduced: (1) Overflow-Aware Scaling, which increases dynamic range under power-of-two block scaling, reducing quantization errors, and (2) Macro Block Scaling, which allocates higher precision scaling to preserve outliers, applied via software changes without modifying hardware.

Result: The methods reduce the MXFP4-NVFP4 accuracy gap from ~10% to under 1% on average, with a modest computational overhead of 6.2%.

Conclusion: MXFP4 is re-established as a competitive alternative to NVFP4 for large-scale LLM inference, combining near-NVFP4 accuracy with better efficiency, such as a 12% area savings in tensor cores.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have intensified the need for low-precision formats that enable efficient, large-scale inference. The Open Compute Project (OCP) Microscaling (MX) standard is attractive due to its favorable hardware efficiency, but its 4-bit variant (MXFP4) lags behind NVIDIA's NVFP4 in accuracy, limiting adoption. We introduce two software-only techniques, Overflow-Aware Scaling (OAS) and Macro Block Scaling (MBS), that improve MXFP4 quantization fidelity without requiring hardware changes. OAS reduces overall errors by increasing effective dynamic range under power-of-two block scaling, while MBS allocates higher-precision scaling at a coarser granularity to better preserve outliers. Across multiple LLMs and standard downstream benchmarks, OAS and MBS reduce the end-to-end accuracy gap between MXFP4 and NVFP4 from about 10% to below 1% on average, while incurring modest GEMM overhead (6.2% on average). These results re-establish MXFP4 as a practical alternative to NVFP4, enabling near-NVFP4 accuracy while retaining MX's hardware-efficiency advantages (e.g., 12% relative area savings in tensor cores).

</details>


### [52] [VeriInteresting: An Empirical Study of Model Prompt Interactions in Verilog Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08715)
*Luca Collini,Andrew Hennesee,Patrick Yubeaton,Siddharth Garg,Ramesh Karri*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper investigates various factors affecting Verilog code generation using diverse language models and prompt engineering techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the interactions between model reasoning, specialization, and prompt strategies in automated Verilog code generation.

Method: Empirical evaluation of small and large language models using controlled factorial design with various prompt engineering methods, including Genetic-Pareto optimization.

Result: Patterns in how different model classes respond to structured prompts and optimizations were identified, alongside generalized and specific trends across models and benchmarks.

Conclusion: Insights were gained into optimizing automated Verilog code generation using the synergy of model capabilities and prompt strategies.

Abstract: Rapid advances in language models (LMs) have created new opportunities for automated code generation while complicating trade-offs between model characteristics and prompt design choices. In this work, we provide an empirical map of recent trends in LMs for Verilog code generation, focusing on interactions among model reasoning, specialization, and prompt engineering strategies. We evaluate a diverse set of small and large LMs, including general-purpose, reasoning, and domain-specific variants. Our experiments use a controlled factorial design spanning benchmark prompts, structured outputs, prompt rewriting, chain-of-thought reasoning, in-context learning, and evolutionary prompt optimization via Genetic-Pareto. Across two Verilog benchmarks, we identify patterns in how model classes respond to structured prompts and optimization, and we document which trends generalize across LMs and benchmarks versus those that are specific to particular model-prompt combinations.

</details>


### [53] [Design Conductor: An agent autonomously builds a 1.5 GHz Linux-capable RISC-V CPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08716)
*The Verkor Team,Ravi Krishna,Suresh Krishna,David Chin*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: Design Conductor (DC) autonomously built working RISC-V CPU (VerCore) from specifications to tape-out GDSII in 12 hours, achieving 1.48 GHz performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To demonstrate the potential of autonomous agents in semiconductor design, aiming for a complete CPU creation from concept to verified layout within a short timeframe.

Method: Utilized DC's frontier models and methodologies starting from specifications, applying RTL implementation, debugging, optimization for timing closure, and backend tool interactions.

Result: DC autonomously designed variations of a RISC-V CPU, achieving timing closure at 1.48 GHz, CoreMark score of 3261, comparable to mid-2011 Intel Celeron SU2300.

Conclusion: DC showcases how autonomous systems can redefine semiconductor design workflows, hinting at future improvements and transformations in chip-making through frontier technologies.

Abstract: Design Conductor (DC) is an autonomous agent which applies the capabilities of frontier models to build semiconductors end-to-end -- that is, from concept to verified, tape-out ready GDSII (layout CAD file). In 12 hours and fully autonomously, DC was able to build several micro-architecture variations of a complete RISC-V CPU (which we dub VerCore) that meet timing at 1.48 GHz (rv32i-zmmul; using the ASAP7 PDK), starting from a 219-word requirements document. The VerCore achieves a CoreMark score of 3261. For historical context, this is roughly equivalent to an Intel Celeron SU2300 from mid-2011 (which ran at 1.2 GHz). To our knowledge, this is the first time an autonomous agent has built a complete, working CPU from spec to GDSII. This report is organized as follows. We first review DC's design and its key components. We then describe the methodology that DC followed to build VerCore -- including RTL implementation, testbench implementation, frontend debugging, optimization to achieve timing closure, and interacting with backend tools. We review the key characteristics of the resulting VerCore. Finally, we highlight how frontier models could improve to better enable this application, and our lessons learned as to how chips will be built in the future enabled by the capabilities of systems like DC.

</details>


### [54] [CktEvo: Repository-Level RTL Code Benchmark for Design Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08718)
*Zhengyuan Shi,Jingxin Wang,Tairan Cheng,Changran Xu,Weikang Qian,Qiang Xu*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces CktEvo, a benchmark and framework for repository-level RTL evolution to improve Power, Performance, and Area (PPA) while maintaining functionality, utilizing closed-loop feedback with large language models (LLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing benchmarks focused on isolated snippets and to enable repository-scale RTL optimization by improving PPA while preserving functionality.

Method: The authors formalize a task where given an initial repository, the objective is to produce edits that improve PPA and maintain functionality. They use a closed-loop framework combining LLM-generated edits and toolchain feedback for iterative refinement.

Result: CktEvo leverages high-quality Verilog repositories, formalizes repository-level RTL optimization, and achieves PPA improvements without requiring human interactions, highlighting the viability of LLM-assisted frameworks.

Conclusion: CktEvo establishes an executable foundation for scalable, function-preserving RTL optimization by leveraging repository-level benchmarks and LLMs, which could significantly contribute to engineering practices focusing on PPA-driven evolution.

Abstract: Register-Transfer Level (RTL) coding is an iterative, repository-scale process in which Power, Performance, and Area (PPA) emerge from interactions across many files and the downstream toolchain. While large language models (LLMs) have recently been applied to hardware design, most efforts focus on generation or debugging from natural-language prompts, where ambiguity and hallucinations necessitate expert review. A separate line of work begins from formal inputs, yet typically optimizes high-level synthesis or isolated modules and remains decoupled from cross-file dependencies. In this work, we present CktEvo, a benchmark and reference framework for repo-level RTL evolution. Unlike prior benchmarks consisting of isolated snippets, our benchmark targets complete IP cores where PPA emerges from cross-file dependencies. Our benchmark packages several high-quality Verilog repositories from real-world designs. We formalize the task as: given an initial repository, produce edits that preserve functional behavior while improving PPA. We also provide a closed-loop framework that couples LLM-proposed edits with toolchain feedback to enable cross-file modifications and iterative repair at repository scale. Our experiments demonstrate that the reference framework realizes PPA improvements without any human interactions. CktEvo establishes a rigorous and executable foundation for studying LLM-assisted RTL optimization that matters for engineering practice: repository-level, function-preserving, and PPA-driven.

</details>


### [55] [SiliconMind-V1: Multi-Agent Distillation and Debug-Reasoning Workflows for Verilog Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08719)
*Mu-Chi Chen,Yu-Hung Kao,Po-Hsuan Huang,Shao-Chun Ho,Hsiang-Yu Tsou,I-Ting Wu,En-Ming Huang,Yu-Kai Hung,Wei-Po Hsin,Cheng Liang,Chia-Heng Tu,Shih-Hao Hung,Hsiang-Tsung Kung*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: A multi-agent framework is introduced to improve Verilog code generation's functional correctness, achieving better results than state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based methods for Verilog code generation focus on syntactic correctness, often relying on commercial models and external tools, posing privacy and cost issues with limited functional correctness.

Method: The proposed multi-agent framework integrates reasoning-oriented training data generation and testbench-driven verification to enable an iterative design process with locally fine-tuned LLMs, SiliconMind-V1.

Result: Experimentation shows the framework outperforms state-of-the-art QiMeng-CodeV-R1 in functional correctness on benchmarks while using fewer training resources.

Conclusion: The framework offers a cost-effective, privacy-conscious, and efficient solution for generating functionally correct Verilog designs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently emerged as a promising approach for automating Verilog code generation; however, existing methods primarily emphasize syntactic correctness and often rely on commercial models or external verification tools, which introduces concerns regarding cost, data privacy, and limited guarantees of functional correctness. This work proposes a unified multi-agent framework for reasoning-oriented training data generation with integrated testbench-driven verification, enabling locally fine-tuned LLMs, SiliconMind-V1, to iteratively generate, test, and debug Register-Transfer Level (RTL) designs through test-time scaling. Experimental results on representative benchmarks (VerilogEval-v2, RTLLM-v2, and CVDP) demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms the state-of-the-art QiMeng-CodeV-R1 in functional correctness while using fewer training resources.

</details>


### [56] [AnalogToBi: Device-Level Analog Circuit Topology Generation via Bipartite Graph and Grammar Guided Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08720)
*Seungmin Kim,Mingun Kim,Yuna Lee,Yulhwa Kim*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces AnalogToBi, a framework for generating device-level analog circuit topologies with high validity, novelty, and structural reasoning capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in analog circuit topology generation, such as lack of functional controllability, over-fitting on training data, and generation of invalid circuits.

Method: The framework introduces a bipartite graph representation to decouple ordering from semantics, grammar-guided decoding for electrical validity, and data augmentation through device renaming.

Result: AnalogToBi achieves 97.8% validity, 92.1% novelty, and 89.9% valid and novel circuits. Generated designs outperform prior methods in SPICE simulations.

Conclusion: AnalogToBi effectively generates innovative and valid analog circuit topologies, advancing both design quality and automation potential.

Abstract: Automatic generation of device-level analog circuit topologies remains a fundamental challenge in analog design automation. Recent transformer-based approaches have shown promise, yet they often suffer from limited functional controllability, memorization of training data, and the generation of electrically invalid circuits. We propose AnalogToBi, a device-level analog circuit topology generation framework that addresses these limitations. AnalogToBi enables explicit functional control via a circuit type token and adopts a bipartite graph-based circuit representation that decouples positional ordering from functional semantics, encouraging structural reasoning over sequence memorization. In addition, grammar-guided decoding enforces electrical validity during generation, while apply device renaming-based data augmentation improves generalization by increasing sequence diversity without altering circuit functionality. Experimental results show that AnalogToBi achieves 97.8% validity and 92.1% novelty, resulting in 89.9% valid and novel circuits under conditional generation, without human expert involvement. We further present that generated circuits can be automatically translated into SPICE netlists, and SPICE simulations confirm that AnalogToBi discovers high-quality analog topologies that outperform prior methods. For code and supplementary materials, see https://github.com/Seungmin0825/AnalogToBi

</details>


### [57] [KernelCraft: Benchmarking for Agentic Close-to-Metal Kernel Generation on Emerging Hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08721)
*Jiayi Nie,Haoran Wu,Yao Lai,Zeyu Cao,Cheng Zhang,Binglei Lou,Erwei Wang,Jianyi Cheng,Timothy M. Jones,Robert Mullins,Rika Antonova,Yiren Zhao*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: KernelCraft introduces a benchmark to assess LLM agents' ability to generate and optimize low-level kernels for new hardware ISAs, demonstrating their efficiency in kernel development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of manually crafting low-level kernels for new hardware ISAs, which is time-consuming, error-prone, and not scalable across diverse hardware platforms.

Method: KernelCraft employs a function-calling, feedback-driven workflow wherein LLM agents refine kernels based on automated feedback, including compilation checks, simulation, and correctness validation against ground truth.

Result: Top agents successfully produce valid and optimized kernels for novel ISAs, outperforming template-based compiler baselines and scaling across diverse hardware targets in KernelCraft's extensive evaluations.

Conclusion: KernelCraft showcases the potential for LLM agents to streamline kernel development processes for emerging accelerators, reducing costs and increasing development efficiency.

Abstract: New AI accelerators with novel instruction set architectures (ISAs) often require developers to manually craft low-level kernels -- a time-consuming, laborious, and error-prone process that cannot scale across diverse hardware targets. This prevents emerging hardware platforms from reaching the market efficiently. While prior LLM-based code generation has shown promise in mature GPU ecosystems, it remains unclear whether agentic LLM systems can quickly produce valid and efficient kernels for emerging hardware with new ISAs. We present KernelCraft: the first benchmark to evaluate an LLM agent's ability to generate and optimize low-level kernels for customized accelerators via a function-calling, feedback-driven workflow. Within KernelCraft, the agent refines kernels under ISA and hardware constraints using automated feedback derived from compilation checks, simulation, and correctness validation against ground truth. In our experiments, we assess agent performance across three emerging accelerator platforms on more than 20 ML tasks, each with 5 diverse task configurations, with special evaluation of task configuration complexity. Across four leading reasoning models, top agents produce functionally valid kernels for previously unseen ISAs within a few refinement steps, with optimized kernels that match or outperform template-based compiler baselines. With that, we demonstrate the potential for reducing the cost of kernel development for accelerator designers and kernel developers.

</details>


### [58] [ALADIN: Accuracy-Latency-Aware Design-space Inference Analysis for Embedded AI Accelerators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08722)
*T. Baldi,D. Casini,A. Biondi*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: ALADIN is a framework for analyzing mixed-precision quantized neural networks targeting embedded AI accelerators by evaluating accuracy, latency, and resource consumption trade-offs without deploying on the platform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of balancing accuracy, latency, and resource constraints in real-time embedded systems running deep neural networks.

Method: ALADIN employs a refinement process to transform QONNX models into platform-aware representations, integrating platform-independent and hardware-specific details. It uses a cycle-accurate simulator for validation.

Result: ALADIN successfully evaluates architectural decisions and mixed-precision quantization strategies, effectively identifying their impact on accuracy, latency, and resource usage in a RISC-V based AI accelerator.

Conclusion: ALADIN is an effective tool for simplifying development and optimizing resource-constrained AI systems by analyzing and balancing accuracy, latency, and resource consumption trade-offs.

Abstract: The inference of deep neural networks (DNNs) on resource-constrained embedded systems introduces non-trivial trade-offs among model accuracy, computational latency, and hardware limitations, particularly when real-time constraints must be satisfied. This paper presents ALADIN, an accuracy-latency-aware design-space inference analysis framework for mixed-precision quantized neural networks (QNNs) targeting scratchpad-based AI accelerators. ALADIN enables the evaluation and analysis of inference bottlenecks and design trade-offs across accuracy, latency, and resource consumption without requiring deployment on the target platform, thereby significantly reducing development time and cost.
  The framework introduces a progressive refinement process that transforms a canonical QONNX model into platform-aware representations by integrating both platform-independent implementation details and hardware-specific characteristics. ALADIN is validated using a cycle-accurate simulator of a RISC-V based platform specialized for AI workloads, demonstrating its effectiveness as a tool for quantitative inference analysis and hardware-software co-design. Experimental results highlight how architectural decisions and mixed-precision quantization strategies impact accuracy, latency, and resource usage, and show that these effects can be precisely evaluated and compared using ALADIN, while also revealing subtle optimization tensions.

</details>


### [59] [PhD Thesis Summary: Methods for Reliability Assessment and Enhancement of Deep Neural Network Hardware Accelerators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08724)
*Mahdi Taheri*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces novel methods for assessing and enhancing reliability in DNN hardware accelerators, develops analytical tools, and proposes a no-overhead reliability method, AdAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliability in DNN hardware accelerators is crucial due to their increasing deployment, demanding cost-effective solutions for reliability, fault tolerance, and computational efficiency.

Method: Conducted a systematic literature review to identify gaps and created new reliability assessment tools. Developed AdAM, a zero-overhead method to boost fault tolerance and optimize trade-offs.

Result: Introduced methods like AdAM with comparable fault tolerance to redundancy techniques but at reduced hardware costs. Outcomes contributed to academic projects, courses, collaborations, and tool development.

Conclusion: This research provides cost-efficient and robust approaches to improve DNN hardware reliability, making significant academic and industrial impacts.

Abstract: This manuscript summarizes the work and showcases the impact of the doctoral thesis by introducing novel, cost-efficient methods for assessing and enhancing the reliability of DNN hardware accelerators. A comprehensive Systematic Literature Review (SLR) was conducted, categorizing existing reliability assessment techniques, identifying research gaps, and leading to the development of new analytical reliability assessment tools. Additionally, this work explores the interplay between reliability, quantization, and approximation, proposing methodologies that optimize the trade-offs between computational efficiency and fault tolerance. Furthermore, a real-time, zero-overhead reliability enhancement technique, AdAM, was developed, providing fault tolerance comparable to traditional redundancy methods while significantly reducing hardware costs. The impact of this research extends beyond academia, contributing to multiple funded projects, masters courses, industrial collaborations, and the development of new tools and methodologies for efficient and reliable DNN hardware accelerators.

</details>


### [60] [Performance Analysis of Edge and In-Sensor AI Processors: A Comparative Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08725)
*Luigi Capogrosso,Pietro Bonazzi,Michele Magno*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper reviews ultra-low-power edge processors, analyzing their architectures, benchmarks, and suitability for AI workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for specialized ultra-low-power edge processors capable of handling latency-critical AI workloads efficiently.
